industries.name: Nuclear Fuel (Potential) & Research
uses: Thorium-232 (²³²Th, t½ = 14.05 billion years) is fertile — it absorbs a neutron to become ²³³U (uranium-233), a fissile fuel. Thorium fuel cycles produce far less long-lived transuranic waste than uranium cycles and are more proliferation-resistant

India's three-stage nuclear program is built around thorium: India has vast thorium reserves (monazite sands) and plans to use PHWR reactors (stage 1) → fast breeder reactors with Pu fuel (stage 2) → thorium-based advanced heavy water reactors (stage 3)

Molten Salt Reactors (MSRs) using liquid thorium fuel: several startups (Terrestrial Energy, Flibe Energy, Copenhagen Atomics) are developing MSRs that dissolve ThF₄ in molten fluoride salt as both fuel and coolant — restarting interest in the Oak Ridge Molten Salt Reactor Experiment (1965–1969)
CANDU reactors can use thorium oxide fuel without major design changes — demonstration thorium fuel bundles have been tested in Indian PHWRs

companies.name: India's Department of Atomic Energy (DAE)
companies.use: The world's most committed institutional actor in thorium nuclear energy. India holds ~25% of world thorium reserves in monazite beach sands (Kerala, Tamil Nadu). BARC (Bhabha Atomic Research Centre) actively develops thorium fuel fabrication and AHWR (Advanced Heavy Water Reactor) designs.

companies.name: Terrestrial Energy
companies.use: Developing the Integral Molten Salt Reactor (IMSR) — a Generation IV reactor using molten fluoride salt with dissolved thorium and uranium fuel. Has received Canadian Nuclear Safety Commission pre-licensing review. Targeting commercial deployment in the 2030s.

industries.name: Gas Mantles & Refractories
uses: Thoriated gas mantles: thorium oxide (ThO₂, 1–2%) in incandescent gas mantles dramatically improves luminous efficiency — ThO₂ catalyzes the combustion reaction and raises the effective emission temperature. Thoriated mantles produce ~30% more light than cerium-only alternatives

Thoriated gas mantles are mildly radioactive due to ²³²Th content and its decay products — regulatory restrictions vary by country; some jurisdictions have banned sale, others permit it for camping and outdoor use

Thorium oxide (ThO₂) as a refractory: melting point 3300°C — the highest of any oxide. Used in high-temperature crucibles, furnace liners, and specialty refractory applications where zirconia and magnesia reach their limits
ThO₂ as catalyst support: historically used in the Wacker process for acetaldehyde production and in steam reforming catalysts — now largely replaced by more sustainable alternatives

companies.name: Humphrey (Coghlan's Ltd.)
companies.share: ~20% of North American gas mantle market
companies.use: Major camping equipment supplier including gas mantles for lanterns. Produces both thoriated and non-thoriated (cerium oxide only) gas mantles for propane and white gas camping lanterns — the thoriated version produces superior light output.

industries.name: Specialty Alloys & Optics
uses: Thoriated tungsten TIG welding electrodes (W-ThO₂, 1–2% ThO₂): the traditional high-performance TIG electrode — thorium lowers the electrode work function, improving arc starting and stability for welding of stainless steel, titanium, copper, and nickel alloys. Being replaced by CeO₂ and La₂O₃ electrodes for regulatory compliance

Thorium-magnesium alloys (Mag-Thor): Mg-Th alloys with improved creep resistance at elevated temperatures, historically used in aerospace applications (Rolls-Royce Viper jet engine components) — now replaced by Mg-rare earth alloys due to thorium's radioactivity

Thoriated optical glass (lanthanum-thorium glass): thorium oxide historically used in high-refractive-index optical glass for camera and microscope lenses — replaced by rare earth-containing glass (La₂O₃, Y₂O₃) after radiation concerns from yellowing of thoriated glass under UV and gamma exposure

Monazite processing: monazite sand (Ce,La,Nd,Th)PO₄ contains 6–12% ThO₂ — processed for rare earth separation produces thorium as a byproduct that must be safely stored, creating a regulatory challenge for rare earth producers

companies.name: Sylvania / Osram (ams-OSRAM)
companies.use: Historically major producer of thoriated tungsten wire and electrodes for lighting and welding. Still produces some thoriated TIG electrodes (EWTh-2) for the welding market where arc performance requirements justify thorium over alternatives.
